title: MinStack (push, pop, min)
logicSummary: A MinStack is implemented using dual stacks or maintaining pairs where one tracks standard values and the other records the minimum up to that point. Pushing and popping manipulate both structures simultaneously to ensure synchronization. This enables retrieving the minimum value at any point directly in O(1) time.

viva.q: How does MinStack ensure O(1) minimum retrieval?
viva.a: By maintaining a secondary stack (or pairs in a single stack) that strictly keeps track of the minimum up to that point alongside the main values.

viva.q: What happens if we push multiple identical minimums?
viva.a: The minimum stack replicates the push to ensure synchronization; when a duplicate minimum pops, the secondary stack still holds the previous minimum safely.

title: Postfix Evaluation
logicSummary: The algorithm processes symbols continuously. By utilizing a stack to hold operands, operators evaluate the topmost items directly. The final result remains as the single element in the stack upon conclusion.

viva.q: Why use a stack for postfix evaluation?
viva.a: A stack perfectly handles the Last-In-First-Out nature required: as operators are encountered, they must be applied to the most recently seen operands.

viva.q: How are operands and operators handled?
viva.a: Operands are pushed onto the stack. Operators pop the top two operands from the stack, evaluate the result, and push the result back onto the stack.

title: Next Greater Element (NGE)
logicSummary: NGE uses a monotonic stack storing indices or elements decreasingly. Scanning right-to-left resolves the next larger item efficiently. Each item enters and exits the stack once, giving linear limits.

viva.q: How does the stack optimize finding the next greater element?
viva.a: It uses a decreasing monotonic stack, keeping track of elements looking for their next greater element. When a larger element is found, it safely pops and updates the result for the smaller elements.

viva.q: What is the worst-case scenario for NGE?
viva.a: A strictly decreasing array, where every element must be pushed onto the stack and scanned entirely without finding a greater element until the end (e.g. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1).

title: Circular Queue
logicSummary: A circular queue uses a fixed-size array and two pointers wrapping around modulo the limit. It resets to the beginning efficiently. This prevents shifting whole arrays upon removing from the front.

viva.q: What is the advantage of a circular queue over a linear one?
viva.a: It prevents the 'false full' condition of linear queues by allowing inserting at the freed-up front spaces dynamically using modulo arithmetic.

viva.q: How is the 'full' condition checked?
viva.a: By checking if `(rear + 1) % capacity == front`. If true, the queue is mathematically full.

title: Infix to Postfix (Shunting Yard)
logicSummary: Operands pass through to the output, and operators flow onto a stack based on precedence rules. Stronger operators resolve before weaker ones. Parentheses guide boundaries explicitly.

viva.q: Why do we need operators precedence?
viva.a: Precedence determines which operator should forcefully resolve (pop) before a weaker operator can be added to the stack, adhering to standardized math rules.

viva.q: How are parentheses processed?
viva.a: Opening parentheses are pushed directly. Closing parentheses pop all operators until their corresponding opening parenthesis is reached.

title: Product of 3 Largest
logicSummary: A min-heap of size exactly 3 continually curates the top elements. Traversing the array filters standard insertions dynamically. The resulting peak elements multiply to give the outcome.

viva.q: Why use a min-heap instead of sorting?
viva.a: A min-heap strictly manages the top 3 highest elements effortlessly in O(n log 3) time, avoiding the heavy O(n log n) overhead of sorting the entire array.

viva.q: Can this be done without extra space?
viva.a: Yes, you can track just three variables (max1, max2, max3) specifically and update them in a single pass manually without a heap structure.

title: Merge Two Sorted LL
logicSummary: Trailing pointers iteratively weave the lowest respective heads of two sorted structures together. It resolves incrementally without reallocating nodes. The tail connects anything remaining entirely at the end.

viva.q: Why is the space complexity O(1) and not O(n+m)?
viva.a: Because we aren't creating new nodes; we're simply rearranging the 'next' pointers between existing nodes of both linked lists to weave them together.

viva.q: How do we handle lists of unequal lengths?
viva.a: Once one list is completely traversed, the remaining tail of the other list is just directly appended to the merged pointer without further comparison.

title: Merge Point of Two LL
logicSummary: A dual-pointer method spans lengths differently so differences resolve on the second pass. If intersection exists, traversing overlaps eventually aligns symmetrically. Space operates efficiently in constant bounds.

viva.q: How does the two-pointer technique work here?
viva.a: Pointers simultaneously traverse both lists. Once a pointer hits the end, it flips to the head of the OTHER list. This synchronizes their lengths on the second pass.

viva.q: What if the lists do not intersect?
viva.a: Both pointers will eventually hit 'null' simultaneously on their matched second pass, breaking the loop efficiently without false positives.

python:
def get_intersection_node(headA, headB):
    if not headA or not headB: return None
    ptrA, ptrB = headA, headB
    while ptrA != ptrB:
        ptrA = ptrA.next if ptrA else headB
        ptrB = ptrB.next if ptrB else headA
    return ptrA

title: Swap Nodes Pairwise (LL)
logicSummary: Pointers hop twice repeatedly along the collection length. Rebinding the intermediate next-pointers handles reversing adjacent sets securely. This executes continuously right to left with dummy-head anchoring.

viva.q: Why swap nodes instead of values?
viva.a: Swapping values involves deep copying data which can be expensive (especially for large objects). Simply re-linking the next pointers is purely O(1) computationally.

viva.q: How do you handle an odd number of nodes?
viva.a: The final single node is simply left as-is, because the loop requires pairs strictly to perform the swapping pointers trick.

python:
def swap_pairs(head):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    dummy.next = head
    curr = dummy
    while curr.next and curr.next.next:
        first = curr.next
        second = curr.next.next
        
        first.next = second.next
        second.next = first
        curr.next = second
        curr = first
    return dummy.next

title: Validate BST
logicSummary: Checking iteratively requires nodes verifying rigid left/right bounded thresholds spanning downward. If thresholds are breached the tree is invalid. In-order checking can instead yield strict monotonic increases.

viva.q: What is the range approach to validate a BST?
viva.a: Recursively pass an updating 'min' and 'max' boundary constraint downwards. If any node's value falls outside its specific inherited range, the tree is fundamentally invalid.

viva.q: Why does inorder traversal work for validation?
viva.a: An in-order traversal of a strict Binary Search Tree specifically guarantees a purely sorted ascending order sequence mathematically.

title: Build BST
logicSummary: Data cascades individually from a root recursively determining left for less and right for more iteratively. Constructive balancing ensures typical log heights remain standard. Worst scenarios yield linear degradation.

viva.q: How do you insert an element in a binary search tree?
viva.a: You start from the root. If the incoming value is smaller, traverse left; if larger, traverse right. Insert it as a new leaf when a null spot is found.

viva.q: What happens if the input is sorted?
viva.a: It aggressively degenerates into a completely skewed, unbalanced right-sided (or left-sided) single linked list, causing O(n) search/insert degradation.

title: Max Depth of Tree
logicSummary: A depth-first or bottom-up protocol measures branches separately and evaluates the greater path recursively. Combining right and left paths dynamically provides the full span. Addition by one resolves root inclusion.

viva.q: How do breadth-first and depth-first approaches compare?
viva.a: DFS recursively adds 1 + max(left, right) cleanly down path branches. BFS queues nodes level-by-level, incrementing a counter completely per layer explicitly.

viva.q: What does a balanced tree's height look like?
viva.a: A perfectly balanced tree natively operates at close to O(log p) depth proportionately to the number of nodes (p).

title: Tree Traversals (In/Pre/Post)
logicSummary: Traversals recursively anchor a sequence pattern around visiting root elements relatively against their spans. An in-order pattern specifically sorts regular BST architectures perfectly. Pre- and post-order manage copy-state and delete-state naturally.

viva.q: Which traversal is used for deleting a tree?
viva.a: Post-order traversal strictly, because naturally you must fully delete child nodes recursively before you blindly delete their parent node safely.

viva.q: How can we do traversals without recursion?
viva.a: By dynamically mimicking the call stack purely using specific explicit stack structures (e.g. while loops) and iterating down paths.

python:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.left = None
        self.right = None


class BuildTree:
    def __init__(self):
        self.idx = 0

    def build_tree(self, arr):
        if self.idx >= len(arr):
            return None

        if  arr[self.idx] == -1:
            self.idx+=1
            return None

        root = Node(arr[self.idx])
        self.idx += 1

        root.left = self.build_tree(arr)
        root.right = self.build_tree(arr)

        return root

    def preorder(self, root):
        if root is None:
            return
        print(root.data, end=" ")
        self.preorder(root.left)
        self.preorder(root.right)

    def inorder(self, root):
        if root is None:
            return
        self.inorder(root.left)
        print(root.data, end=" ")
        self.inorder(root.right)

    def postorder(self, root):
        if root is None:
            return
        self.postorder(root.left)
        self.postorder(root.right)
        print(root.data, end=" ")


import sys
arr = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
bt = BuildTree()
root = bt.build_tree(arr)
bt.preorder(root)
print()
bt.inorder(root)
print()
bt.postorder(root)

title: Boundary Traversal
logicSummary: Boundary paths consist uniquely of a root, right bound, left bound explicitly, and all subsequent leaves. Combining sequence lists in alternating traverse formats correctly manages non-repeated elements universally. It specifically restricts inner non-leaf subtrees effectively.

viva.q: How do we pick boundary nodes to avoid duplication?
viva.a: By carefully restricting the separate logical passes: extracting the left edge, then explicitly checking leaves, then extracting the right edge backwards without redundant overlaps.

viva.q: What constitutes the boundary of a tree?
viva.a: Logically, it includes the vertical left-sided boundary, all strict bottom leaves sequentially, and the vertical right-sided boundary natively.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)
logicSummary: Navigating checks spanning roots systematically isolates intersection points dynamically over structural boundaries. On BST arrays, evaluating numerical divergence solves it directly. Otherwise paths track nodes confirming common ancestral lines.

viva.q: How is LCA in a BST different from a standard binary tree?
viva.a: In a BST, you can blindly track down. The first node naturally having a value strictly *between* the two targets is mathematically guaranteed to be the LCA.

viva.q: What happens if both nodes are on the same side of the root?
viva.a: The LCA is simply whichever of the two nodes mathematically sits proportionately higher structurally in the path tree.

title: Mirror Trees (Symmetric)
logicSummary: Mirror logic validates parallel structures comparing opposed sides structurally equal but horizontally inverted correctly. A recursive check continuously steps pairs effectively confirming opposite alignment natively. Any irregularity immediately evaluates falsely.

viva.q: What are the checks required to prove two trees are mirrors?
viva.a: You concurrently verify `node1.left == node2.right` and `node1.right == node2.left` while confirming both values perfectly match repeatedly.

viva.q: Can symmetry be checked iteratively?
viva.a: Yes, using a queue (like BFS) and pushing paired nodes constantly to evaluate opposites sequentially without deep recursion.

title: Basic Hash Function
logicSummary: Typical modular hashing evaluates strings and numbers distributing identifiers proportionally across limits. Combining arrays directly indexes into the allocated set appropriately quickly. High quality mappings distribute bits reducing immediate linear overlap universally.

viva.q: What makes a good hash function?
viva.a: It structurally minimizes unpredictable collisions, natively distributes items uniformly within bounds, and evaluates computationally quickly.

viva.q: Common ways to avoid basic collisions?
viva.a: By increasing array bucket size using prime numbers, utilizing mathematical chaining, and integrating linear or quadratic probing specifically.

title: Separate Chaining
logicSummary: Colliding keys occupy the specific array index but resolve dynamically into consecutive linked structures uniformly. Traversal strictly limits checking length proportionally around overall usage metrics directly. Insertion efficiently drops into chains instantaneously natively.

viva.q: Why is linked list used for separate chaining?
viva.a: It natively prevents data loss by appending dynamically expanding node lists into a specific collision bucket infinitely if necessary.

viva.q: How does load factor affect performance?
viva.a: A high load factor proportionately causes longer average overlapping chains, directly increasing average lookup times uncomfortably toward O(n).

title: Two Sum
logicSummary: Mapping individual lookups immediately isolates complements natively traversing single paths linearly easily. Unmatched items push sequentially directly onto maps preserving state effectively. It optimizes away heavy combinatorial checking natively efficiently.

viva.q: Why is hashing better than the two-pointer approach here?
viva.a: The two-pointer technique strictly requires an explicitly sorted array (incurring an O(n log n) cost). Hashing simply works on unsorted arrays naturally in pure O(n).

viva.q: What if the array is already sorted?
viva.a: If sorted, the two-pointer technique runs equally essentially in O(n) and utilizes flawless strictly O(1) space explicitly.

python:
class TwoSum:
    def find_indices(self, arr, target):
        hashmap = {}
        print(f"Finding two indices whose values add up to {target}...")
        for i in range(len(arr)):
            x=arr[i]
            y = target - x
            if y in hashmap:
                print(f"Indices: {hashmap[y]} {i}")
                return
            hashmap[arr[i]] = i
        print("No solution found")

n = int(input().strip())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))
target = int(input().strip())

finder = TwoSum()
finder.find_indices(arr, target)

title: Trie (Search/Insert/Remove)
logicSummary: Trie inserts build node sequences correlating directly to individual characters recursively structurally precisely. Valid completions flag nodes confirming complete word alignments cleanly. Branch paths efficiently reduce string sets naturally effectively.

viva.q: Why use a Trie instead of a hash table?
viva.a: A Trie flawlessly permits prefix searches natively, natively solves structural overlaps dynamically, and optimizes character clustering cleanly without hard hashing conflicts.

viva.q: How does prefix search work?
viva.a: Iteratively stepping down character sequences dynamically evaluating valid boolean terminating flags structurally precisely identifies matched roots.

title: Huffman Coding
logicSummary: Weights map individually driving min-heaps constructing shortest representations dynamically effectively structurally consistently. Heaviest characters sit proportionally closest to the roots effectively. Leaf paths represent optimized bits universally.

viva.q: Why is a min-heap essential for Huffman Coding?
viva.a: It continually retrieves the explicitly two smallest weighted characters dynamically quickly strictly merging them mathematically, rebuilding frequency patterns accurately.

viva.q: What type of data compression does it provide?
viva.a: Lossless prefix-free specifically, strictly optimizing sequence bits based purely on usage-frequency probabilities naturally.

python:
import heapq

class Node:
    def __init__(self, ch, freq):
        self.ch = ch
        self.freq = freq
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

    # heap comparison
    #__lt__ means "less than" (in-built dunder method)
    #here __lt__ is used to compare the Node-objects
    def __lt__(self, other):    
        if self.freq == other.freq:
            return self.ch < other.ch
        return self.freq < other.freq

class HuffmanCoding:
    def __init__(self, chars, freq):
        self.chars = chars
        self.freq = freq
        self.root = None
        self.codes = {}

    def buildTree(self):
        heap = []
        for i in range(len(self.chars)):
            heapq.heappush(heap, Node(self.chars[i], self.freq[i]))

        while len(heap) > 1:
            left = heapq.heappop(heap)
            right = heapq.heappop(heap)
            merged = Node('$', left.freq + right.freq)
            merged.left = left
            merged.right = right
            heapq.heappush(heap, merged)
        self.root = heap[0]

    def generateCodes(self, node, code):
        if not node:
            return
        if node.left is None and node.right is None:
            self.codes[node.ch] = code
            return
        self.generateCodes(node.left, code + "0")
        self.generateCodes(node.right, code + "1")

    def display(self):
        print("Huffman Codes:")
        for ch in self.chars:
            print(f"{ch}: {self.codes[ch]}")

#Main────────────────────────────────────────
n = int(input())
chars = input().split()
freq = list(map(int, input().split()))

huffman = HuffmanCoding(chars, freq)

huffman.buildTree()
huffman.generateCodes(huffman.root, "")
huffman.display()

title: Distinct Substrings
logicSummary: Iteratively sliding structural sequences continuously populates specific branch mappings directly precisely correctly. Suffix architectures inherently evaluate duplicate patterns overlapping naturally identically universally. Finding branches resolves unique sequences proportionally clearly effectively.

viva.q: How does a Trie improve finding distinct substrings compared to hashing?
viva.a: Inserting every suffix mathematically inherently covers every substring structurally. Tries intrinsically filter identical string fragments simultaneously overlapping natively.

viva.q: What is the worst-case space complexity?
viva.a: The Trie effectively grows to O(n^2) branches structurally purely based on entirely dissimilar substring overlaps mathematically uniquely.

title: No. of Words in a Trie
logicSummary: Simple traversal correctly aggregates boolean flags validating termination specifically cleanly directly universally. Root iteration accumulates entirely validating sub-trees proportionately logically simply. Sequence totals calculate automatically precisely.

viva.q: How do you count nodes that represent the end of words?
viva.a: By recursively tracking and aggregating specifically a boolean property `isEnd` attached exactly to leaf or junction node paths strictly explicitly.

viva.q: Can this be done iteratively?
viva.a: Yes, by systematically running a BFS queue natively inspecting every struct explicitly evaluating terminating counts independently quickly.

title: Left View of Tree
logicSummary: First nodes appearing strictly natively across any depths automatically define left views structurally visually effectively. Queue processing limits recording first elements reliably quickly. Equivalently recursive passing establishes maximal limits natively precisely.

viva.q: How does level mapping help in identifying the left view?
viva.a: It restricts queue passing explicitly to record uniquely the mathematically first processed entry of every specific horizontal visual layer physically.

viva.q: Why might DFS be better suited than BFS here?
viva.a: DFS natively passes a physical depth counter cleanly, automatically saving the first encountered node proportionately tracking deeper branches organically efficiently.

title: Level Order Traversal
logicSummary: Queue lists establish flat linear passing accurately systematically level by level completely perfectly carefully. Popped architectures record nodes directly uniformly appropriately systematically clearly universally. Widths evaluate equivalently smoothly dynamically natively.

viva.q: Why use a queue for level order traversal?
viva.a: A queue structurally forces a First-In-First-Out explicitly processing layers sequentially cleanly before reaching structurally deeper layers naturally inherently.

viva.q: How do you preserve level boundaries?
viva.a: By specifically evaluating the queue length strictly natively before dequeueing children loops mathematically completely.

python:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.left = None
        self.right = None
class BinaryTree:
    def __init__(self):
        self.idx = 0
    def build_tree(self, arr):
        if self.idx >= len(arr):
            return None
        if arr[self.idx] == -1:
            self.idx+=1
            return None
        root = Node(arr[self.idx])
        self.idx += 1
        root.left = self.build_tree(arr)
        root.right = self.build_tree(arr)
        return root

    def level_order(self, root):
        q = []
        q.append(root)
        result = []
        while q:
            node = q.pop(0)
            result.append((node.data))
            if node.left:
                q.append(node.left)
            if node.right:
                q.append(node.right)
        print(*result)

import sys
arr = []
arr = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
bt = BinaryTree()
root = bt.build_tree(arr)
bt.level_order(root)
